clean_story:
Earthquake whispers. Tsunami shadows.

The phrase "animal sixth sense" comes from behaviors so sharp they seem supernatural—yet rely on physics humans overlook.

A toad’s skin isn’t just for breathing. Before a 2009 Italian earthquake, 96% of male toads abandoned their breeding site days early. Their skin detects subtle ionic shifts: stressed rocks release charged particles, turning air into a faint electric soup. To them, it’s like smelling lightning before the storm.

Elephants hear with their feet. Infrasound—waves below 20 hertz, inaudible to us—travels faster through ground than air. A 2004 tsunami? Herds fled inland hours ahead, stomping at vibrations like drummers sensing a distant bassline. Their padded feet act as seismic tuners.

Birds use the sky’s GPS. Before tornadoes, homing pigeons refuse to fly. The culprit? Infrared radiation from warming storm clouds disrupts their magnetoreception—internal compasses tuned to Earth’s magnetic field. It’s like a phone losing signal near a microwave.

Sharks desert coasts before hurricanes. Their ampullae of Lorenzini—gel-filled pores—detect temperature drops as deep as 0.01 Celsius per minute. A Category 5 storm’s cold upwellings are a dinner bell… ringing empty.

Three related subjects are magnetic field migration, bioelectric communication, and human early-warning systems.
